segments.text: Hello, everyone. My name is Fanos and I am one of the co-founders of a company called
2050 Materials. We're really excited and happy to be here.
2050 Materials, as you'll see through our presentation, is a data company that focuses
on sustainability data that can be embedded within design workflows. Today we're going
to walk you through an AGS integration of a sustainability agent that does a bunch of
things that we think are really useful. But before I do that, I'll pass to my colleague,
Nicodemus, to do an introduction before I continue.
Sure, thank you, Fanos. Hi, everyone. Nicodemus, the head of data and technology at 2050 Materials.
And in this talk, I'll take you through the more technical sites of our presentation. Thank
you.
Super. So what we do at 2050 Materials is we gather a lot of sustainability data and
our typical customer or user of this data is mostly specialists. But what we are really
passionate about is finding ways to get this data accessible and usable by non-specialists.
And this demo exactly talks about that. So we think that designers should just continue
designing. They don't need to learn another tool or another workflow. And unfortunately,
when we talk about carbon analysis, there's a lot of specific maths that you need to know
or specific conventions that you need to follow. So our goal is not to change how designers
work. It's more to embed this sort of sustainability intelligence in their workflow.
Now I'm going to do the first thing of jumping right into the demo because I think that's
really the most interesting part. Before I go into the demo, just so that you understand
what we're trying to do, this is a chat interface as you see. So similar to something like
a chat GPT that a lot of you might be used to using by now. But the idea is not that
it needs to be a separate application. You could imagine this being, for example, a chat
within your Revit work flow. If I just switch to the demo, as you see, it says, hi, I'm
your 2050 Material agent. You can upload an IFC file. You can also upload a Revit file.
It doesn't really matter. I will select one of the files that we have. Now what you see
here is we load up the 3D design. So I see a little super simple model has 44 items here.
There's a few things I can do. So we have this command called slash help, which allows you
to understand what kind of topics you can work on. And what we really like is this slash
command called match. So this essentially goes through the elements of the 3D design. And it
tries to identify the best carbon emission factor for each element. So it does essentially
all of the intelligence that the sustainability consultant would do for you. So let's just run
it, see how that works. So the result here is 44 or 43 out of 44 items that have been matched.
You can see examples like a wall assembly that's within the design is matched to an item on
our database that's called meeting weight concrete masonry units. And each of these has a
carbon emission factor. So this is great. We know we've matched every single thing that typically
takes quite a long time. So anything from a few hours to a few days. And then what it gets
really interesting is you can do slash report, which gives you the embodied carbon assessment of
the project. So you can really see, you know, which parts contribute the highest to the project.
You can see specific recommendations, the highest, the highest components that would be a good
leverage to work on and things like that. So this again is the whole idea is I'm working on my
project. I can continue working on the 3D model. And as I expand the information, I can just go
back into this chatbot, throw it in, run the slash commands, and then get the updates of my report.
And another final command I want to show, which I think is really nice, is I can generate template
emails for my team, which take into account the carbon calculation that has happened and then
turn them into something I can send my team as a recommendation on what to work on. So this
uses AI to draft an email to my team that says, hi, we've done the analysis for this IFC file.
The total carbon emissions are this much. And here's the three spots that we should be working on
if we want to reduce them. And again, this is what we're trying to avoid here is me as a designer.
If I have no sustainability knowledge specifically, I don't want to have to go back to the consultant,
get their input and then go back to my customer. So it sort of frees me out to continue doing my
design work and ideally end up with something that's really more impactful.
I will now go back to the presentation and I'll let Nikodin speak a little bit about how this all
works on the food. Thank you, Fanos. So Fanos showed you the agent in action. Let me walk you
through what's happening under the hood. So there are six stages fully automated. What used to
take on Salzan weeks now, around in minutes. The first part is suggestion. We accept beam files like
the IFC you just saw in the demo, but also excel based bill of quantities and soon PDFs, whatever the
project team has. The second step is preprocessing. This is where the real challenge starts. Every model
is structured differently. So we need to extract the components and their context before anything else
can happen. The third step is extraction. An LLM classifies every component into product type,
material type, building application with a confidence score. After that step, we have matching where we
use text similarity statistical methods to search across the database for candidates. Finally,
we have the boosting which is the key component because now we feedback the classification
from the extraction part. It does the intelligence layers. And the last thing is a report now that we
have the the matches we can create the carbon assessment and the benchmarks. Fanos will walk you
through this later. Let's go to the next slide. One of the most important aspects of how this
works is the agent file processing. The problem here is quite complex. You just saw Fanos uploaded
a beam model with a few components, but it could be 1000. You can't just throw that into an LLM.
It's too slow, too expensive. You'd lose all the structure and context. And in reality,
we have built use with 10,000 roads, 30 sheets. So and every firm builds that differently.
We build a deterministic extraction pipeline using pattern matching, positional heuristics,
and only fall back to LLMs if that's needed. And we can process, you know, long files in under a
second. So on the left, we handle multiple input types. Pin files, element extraction,
properties, et cetera, be a use. So the system detects the structure. Then we have the three stages.
First, extract the elements and identify what's what. Then map the context, which section,
which floor, which building system does each component belong to. And finally,
an assemble, clean structure items with flexibility. On the right, what comes out is something
the LLM can actually work with. A normalized description with quantity and full context.
Like this concrete slab, we know it's 250 square meters. And it sits in the upper floor structure.
At the bottom, cart, and this is one shot process, the agent valid this is an one shot process.
The agent validates the output adjusts when something looks off and refines it. So this process,
it's iterative. And that's why it's a genetic.
Okay, so let's move to the next slide. So let's talk a little bit about material intelligence.
This is the core intelligence loop. Let me walk you through it with a real example.
So let's take, let's say we start with the raw description from a beam file, saying 150 millimeter
C, reinforcement, concrete, floor slab, C3037. The LLM does three things. First, it cleans up the name
and generates an optimized search query. That's what actually gets sent to the matching engine.
Then the second, it classifies into product type concrete slab with 0.85 confidence, materials
concrete with 95 95 percent confidence building application is floors. These are independent
probabilities. The model can be confident about the material, but uncertain about the product type,
for example. Third, it extracts and explicit technical specs, for example, the thickness,
the strength class, and this is as far as pros. Finally, the matchbox. The search query goes
to the text similarity matching across our full database and gets a raw score. In this case, 0.72.
Now, we combine the match score with the classification using boosting. Here, all the classification
metadata fits back into the boosting step. If the building application matches, if the material
type aligns, each one leaves the score so that from 0.72 it goes to 0.94. So we are very confident
that this is the proper match. You can see the bottom with the bars, the effect of the boosting,
the biggest lifts are when the description is vague, but the classification code text is strong.
Let's move to the last slide. Why are you in the work flows? Why not the single pass matching?
Why doesn't that work? For simple components, for example, if it's concrete, C-30,
37, you can look straight to the database and done. But then we have more complex cases. We have
items that are composites. A wall is not a material. There is metal stats plus
plus the board plus insulation. You have to decompose it into three separate matches.
Context dependent. The same insulation means completely different products depending on whether
it's an external wall or an internal wall. So the context is vital here.
So closing, we're building this because the problem demands it. Construction data is generally
very messy. So every component needs a different approach. And without a hand back to Thanos,
to show you what happens with the carbon numbers.
Super. Thank you. So again, when we talk about carbon assessments, the current process is not what
we think needs to happen long-term. Hence the work that we're doing around the genetic workflows,
hence also the explanation of this demo and all of the stuff that Nicol was presented.
So the whole theory behind this whole presentation as well as the demo that we showed is that we want
to move away from the old narrative where we have a sustainability consultant that needs to be
trained in a very specific tool where they receive project information. They go into a room. They
spend a couple of weeks and come back with a report to something where this process of processing
data, and mapping it to the relevant data points, and then interpreting that information happens
on the fly during the design process. Now besides dropping consultant costs, which probably all
projects could benefit from, what this does long-term is something much bigger. So it allows us to make
decisions that lead to better carbon emissions on our projects without actually having to
wait too long. Because often what happens with the consultants is the fact that we've waited a
couple of weeks for this report and assessment essentially means that the project has moved on.
So a lot of decisions have already been locked in, and even if we know what we need to do to reduce
emissions, we might not be able to do them anymore. Whereas if you integrate this intelligence within
the design workflow, then there's a much higher chance of being able to move faster and make
decisions that actually lead to better outcomes. Now what we are today, Nikodin was presented briefly
where we are. So we have an IFC upload and a 3D viewer. We normalize and we have these agents that
do matching based on the large datasets that we provide. We also do carbon calculations. What
you've shown here is in the early stages or in the manufacturing stages of emissions, but we
actually are able to do carbon emission calculations across all life cycle stages. And then we have some
interactive charts, an AI chat that you can talk to in some PDF reports. What we're working on next,
and this is very relevant also to the sustainability data API, is we really want to integrate
APIs of the APS environment so that you can very easily build these sort of solutions within APS.
We want to have comparison of design options next to each other so you can track the process
and the progress of the project. And then we want to also improve the process of having the
human in the loop. So we do not think that computers will fully do this process and we should
be completely uninvolved. So we are working on finding ways of figuring out where as humans we need
to be involved in the process to really understand and be helped basically to make better decisions here.
Yeah, the closing statement I would do is our goal as I said in the beginning is every designer
that's working on Autodesk solutions or even elsewhere for that matter should be able to
deliver low-carbon projects. We think they should be able to do that without being a sustainability
specialist without necessarily having a team of consultants on the side. And we are super bullish
on using AI and structured data to make it possible. I hope this demo was kind of an example of that.
Thank you very much. Yeah, really happy to take any questions and thanks again for having us.
